Case study · Factorial HR · 2021–2023

Turning time management into the growth engine of an HR platform

How I led product and design across three core HR products and connected them to Payroll to create a unified ecosystem that scaled from 2,500 to 7,000 companies.

My role
Head of Product & Design
Team
50 people
Products
Time Tracking · Time Off · Payroll
Timeline
2021–2023
7,000
Companies using Time Management
(up from 2,500)
300K
Employees tracked
on the platform
+35%
Adoption across the
HR & Payroll ecosystem
01
Context
Three early-stage products.
One strategic opportunity.

Three early-stage products.
One strategic opportunity.

Factorial is an all-in-one HR platform competing in a crowded European market. When I joined as Head of Product and Design, I inherited three products that were functional but early: Time Tracking, Time Off, and Scheduling. Each had been built by separate teams, with its own logic and design language.

In the HR software market, Payroll is the highest-value product in the category. Factorial had a Payroll product, but it was largely manual HR admins had to export time data, reconcile it in spreadsheets, and re-import results every single cycle.

"The data to run payroll existed. It just lived in three different products and reached the finance team through emails and spreadsheets."

My mandate: grow the three core products into something genuinely useful, then connect them to Payroll. Grow first. Connect second.

02
The challenge
Products that worked in isolation
for users who needed them together.

Products that worked in isolation for users who needed them together.

For employees

Clocking in, requesting time off, checking balances: inconsistent, underdeveloped. The basics weren't reliable enough to trust.

For managers

Reviewing timesheets and approving absences required jumping between views with no unified picture. Errors were common.

For HR admins

Closing payroll meant exporting time data, calculating in Excel, and importing back. Days of work, risk every cycle.

For the business

Time management was growing but not converting into Payroll adoption. Products weren't creating pull toward the higher-value part of the platform.

03
How I worked
Decisions made close
to customers and data.

Leading a team of 50
close to customers and data.

I managed a team of 50 people across product and design, spread across multiple product lines. One of the first decisions I made was to restructure around outcomes rather than features each squad owned a problem, not a roadmap item. That changed how we prioritised, how we measured success, and how we talked to stakeholders.

I also made a deliberate call early on: I would stay hands-on in the design work, not just review it. On a platform this complex, the quality of design decisions depends on understanding the system and I could not delegate that fully.

Stakeholder alignment

Time Management sat between HR, Finance, and Engineering. I ran monthly cross-functional reviews to keep all three aligned on priorities and tradeoffs, and to push back when business pressure conflicted with what customers actually needed.

Team structure

I reorganised the team midway to reduce handoffs between Time Tracking, Time Off, and Scheduling. Separate squads had created inconsistency. Consolidating around the HR admin workflow was the right call.

Prioritisation tradeoffs

We had more problems than capacity. I deprioritised Scheduling features for two quarters to get Time Tracking reliable enough that customers trusted it. Unpopular internally, but it proved right.

Customer proximity

Direct sessions with HR managers, admins, and employees. I made sure the team heard customers directly, not through filtered summaries. It changed what we built and how fast we moved.

As-is process map: the HR admin's monthly payroll closing. The Excel reconciliation step was the single biggest source of errors and time loss.

04
Time Tracking
From basic clock-in tool
to the manager's source of truth.

From basic clock-in tool
to the manager's source of truth.

The central design decision: make time data legible at a glance. Three concepts estimated, worked, balance. No more opening individual records to spot a problem.

Manager view: monthly timesheet with estimated, worked, and balance per employee. Colour-coded bars make anomalies visible at a glance.

Each column expands on hover with detailed breakdowns.

05
Scheduling
Shifts as the input
that makes everything else work.

Planning and tracking
as one continuous flow.

Before, Scheduling lived as a separate product with no connection to what happened in Time Tracking. Managers planned shifts in one place and tracked time in another. The disconnect was constant expected hours had to be entered manually, which meant reconciliation errors before a single minute of actual overtime was calculated.

The decision was to rebuild Scheduling as the upstream input for Time Tracking, not a standalone tool. When a manager published a shift grid, those planned hours automatically populated the "estimated" column in the timesheet. Planning became the source of truth. Tracking became the measurement against it.

Scheduling: weekly shift grid per employee. Published shifts feed directly into the estimated hours calculation in Time Tracking no manual entry, no reconciliation step.

06
Time Off
From a request form
to a policy engine.

From a request form
to a policy engine.

Companies have different absence types, different approval chains, different rules for different teams. We rebuilt the admin experience around a clear configuration model: policies first, blocked periods next, absence types last.

Dashboard: pending requests with overdue alerts, absence breakdown by type, and weekly trend across offices.

Left: absence types and policy configuration. Right: the review modal that managers use to approve or reject requests.

07
Connecting to Payroll
The loop closes.

The loop closes: time worked
becomes payroll, automatically.

With both products working reliably, we built the first bridge: overtime compensation. When a manager approved extra hours, they could immediately decide pay via a payroll supplement, or convert to time off.

⏱

Time worked

Hours tracked and scheduled

✓

Approval

Timesheet reviewed by manager

⇄

Decision

Overtime routed to pay or time off

€

Supplement

Auto-created in payroll period

One decision routes extra hours to a payroll supplement or time off balance.

Payroll preparation: supplements auto-created from Time Tracking, traceable back to the original timesheet.

08
Outcome
What the work
actually moved.

From three separate products
to a platform people relied on.

Over two years, Time Tracking went from a basic clock-in tool to the manager's primary source of truth. Time Off went from a simple request form to a configurable policy engine that HR admins could own independently. Scheduling became the upstream input that made everything else work.

When we connected them to Payroll, the impact was immediate. Admins who used to spend three to five days reconciling spreadsheets every month could close payroll in a fraction of the time. The Excel step disappeared. The risk disappeared with it.

7,000
Companies using Time Management
up from 2,500 when I joined
+35%
Adoption across the HR
& Payroll ecosystem
300K
Employees tracked
on the platform

Payroll adoption among Time Tracking customers grew significantly once the connection was live. The platform started creating the retention and contract value that made it genuinely competitive in the European HR market.

09
Growth & retention
Reducing churn and
iterating with data.

The hardest growth work
is keeping the customers you have.

A significant part of the work at Factorial was not new features but retention. We had legacy customers at risk of churning because the product had moved on without them. I led the design work to close that gap: understanding what those customers needed that we had not built, prioritising the features with the highest retention value, and getting them shipped.

In Time Management specifically, the features that moved retention metrics were not the headline ones. Reports, shift swaps, and break management were the gaps that HR admins flagged repeatedly. Getting those right required going back to customers, understanding their operational reality, and designing flows that fit how they actually ran their teams.

Churn analysis

We worked with CX and data to identify which customers were at risk and what product gaps were driving their dissatisfaction. That analysis directly shaped our roadmap for Time Management features.

A/B experimentation

We ran A/B tests on feature changes across the platform, testing different interaction patterns, flows, and UI changes against activation and engagement metrics. I worked with product to define hypotheses and interpret results.

Onboarding

The onboarding experience covered importing employee data via Excel, guided setup flows, and direct support from CX. I designed the flows that took a new customer from signup to their first payroll period with confidence.

Customer feedback loops

We had direct lines to customers through CX and regular product interviews. Retention features like shift swaps and break management came directly from those conversations, not from internal assumptions.

10
Learnings

What I'd do differently
and what I'd do the same.

01
Mature before you connect
Getting Time Tracking and Time Off to a level customers genuinely relied on was what made the Payroll connection meaningful. An integration built on weak products just creates a more complex version of the same problem.
02
Integration is a product decision, not an engineering one
The payroll connection only worked because we started from a user question: "What does a manager need to decide when they approve overtime?" That framing made the right solution obvious.
03
Consistency is a growth lever
Investing in shared patterns the same approval flow, the same table conventions paid off. The best feedback we got was: "It feels like one product now."
04
The team structure is a design decision
How you organise the team shapes what gets built and how fast. Reorganising around the HR admin's end-to-end workflow instead of separate product lines reduced the inconsistency that was the biggest user complaint. Organisation design and product design are the same problem.
